California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 2, 1986

Christo D. Bardis
Attorney at Law
9985 Folsom Boulevard
Sacramento, CA 95827

Re: Your Request for Advice,
Our File No. A-85-269

Dear Mr. Bardis:

You have written to us with regard to the following facts
as stated in your letter:

FACTS

The Western Standardbred Association is a nonprofit
Delaware Corporation doing business in California. The
California Horse Racing Board recognizes that Association
(WSA) as the exclusive bargaining agent for the
Standardbred Racing (Harness) Industry. Since 1983 the WSA
has withdrawn monies from the purse pool for political
purposes. From 1983 to July of 1984 the following
provision appeared in the WSA's Articles of Incorporation:

'No part of the net earnings of the corporation shall
inure to the benefit of or be distributable to members,
directors, officers, or other private persons, except that
the corporation shall be authorized and empowered to pay
reasonable compensation for services rendered and to make
payments and distributions in furtherance of the purposes
set forth in Article Third hereof. No substantial part of
the activities of the corporation shall be the carrying on
of propaganda, or otherwise attempting, to influence
legislation, and the corporation shall not participate in,
or intervene in (including the publishing or distribution
of statements) any political campaign on behalf of any
candidate for public office. Notwithstanding any other
provision of these articles, the corporation shall not
carry on any other activities not permitted to be carried
on by a corporation exempt from Federal income tax under

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Christo D. Bardis
January 2, 1986
Page 2


section 501(c)(6) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1954 (or
the corresponding provision of any future United States
Internal Revenue Law).'

In July of 1984, as a Director of that corporation, I
forced the Board to remove that provision. To date I have
been unsuccessful in getting the WSA to refrain from taking
money from the purse pool and giving the monies to a
self-serving pack called the Harness Horsemen's Political
Action Committee, who in turn distribute the funds to
politicians. I find this conduct appalling and in
violation of the First Amendment. These contributions are
not voluntary, they are taken from the purse pool prior to
distribution to owners thereby making the contribution tax
deductible.

The California Business and Professional Code,
Chapter 4, Division 8, Section 19613(c) allows deductions
from the purse pool for 'administrative expenses and
services rendered' with respect to conduct of racing
meetings. These expenditures made are not for
administrative expenses and services rendered as it relates
to a race meeting and, therefore, may be illegal and in
violation of statutes that govern your organization.

QUESTION

You have basically requested that our agency "investigate"
the foregoing situation to determine if the provisions of the
Political Reform Act-1/ have been violated.

ADVICE

The questions raised by your letter go to the propriety of
a nonprofit, tax-exempt corporation using money which it
receives from the "purse pool" for political action purposes.
The Political Reform Act does not govern questions of this
nature. It is principally a reporting statute. So long as the
source of the funds is properly identified and the expenditures
are accurately reported, no issues are raised under our
statute. However, you may wish to contact the various taxing
authorities with respect to the tax-exemption issues raised by
your correspondence.

___________________________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified.

Christo D. Bardis
January 2, 1986
Page 3


     Should you conclude that you believe the reporting
requirements of the Political Reform Act have been violated,
you should address your complaint to the Commission's
Enforcement Division.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:nwm

CHRISTO D. BARDIS
Attorney at Law
9985 Folsom Boulevard
Sacramento, California 95827
(916) 366-3665


December 23, 1985


John Keplinger, Director
Fair Political Practice Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, CA. 95814

Dear Mr. Keplinger:

     The Western Standardbred Association is a nonprofit
Delaware Corporation doing business in California.  The
California Horse Racing Board recognizes that Association
(WSA) as the exclusive bargaining agent for the Standard-
bred Racing (Harness) Industry.  Since 1983 the WSA has
withdrawn monies from the purse pool for political purposes.
From 1983 to July of 1984 the following provision appeared
in the WSA's Articles of Incorporation:

     "No part of the net earnings of the corporation shall
inure to the benefit of or be distributable to its members,
directors, officers, or other private persons, except that
the corporation shall be authorized and empowered to pay
reasonable compensation for services rendered and to make
payments and distributions in furtherance of the purposes
set forth in Article Third hereof.  No substantial part of
the activities of the corporation shall be the carrying on
of propaganda, or otherwise attempting, to influence legis-
lation, and the corporation shall not participate in, or
intervene in (including the publishing or distribution of
statements) any political campaign on behalf of any candi-
date for public office.  Notwithstanding any other pro-
vision of these articles, the corporation shall not carry
on any other activities not permitted to be carried on by a
corporation exempt from Federal income tax under section
501(c)(6) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1954 (or the
corresponding provision of any future United States Inter-
nal Revenue Law)."

     In July of 1984, as a Director of that corporation, I
forced the Board to remove that provision.  To date I have
been unsuccessful in getting the WSA to refrain from taking
money from the purse pool and giving the monies to a
self-serving pack called the Harness Horsemen's Political
Action Committee, who in turn distribute the funds to
politicians.  I find this conduct appalling and in violation
of the First Amendment.  These contributions are not volun-
tary, they are taken from the purse pool prior to distribu-
tion to owners thereby making the contribution tax deductible.

-2-                                December 23, 1985


     The California Business and Professional Code, Chapter
4, Division 8, Section 19613(c) allows deductions from the
purse pool for "administrative expenses and services
rendered" with respect to conduct of racing meetings.
These expenditures made are not for administrative expenses
and services rendered as it relates to a race meeting and,
therefore, may be illegal and in violation of statutes that
govern your organization.

     I am enclosing for your review payments to politicians
and lobbyists entitled Exhibit "A", and communications sent
to the California Racing Board and the President of WSA.

     I would hope that your office would immediately
investigate this apparent illegal act and that "cease and
desist" orders would be issued to the Racing Commission and
WSA prohibiting this type of conduct.

     If I can be of assistance, please notify me.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Christo D. Bardis

EXHIBIT "A"

sections.question:
You have basically requested that our agency "investigate" the foregoing situation to determine if the provisions of the Political Reform Act1/ have been violated.

ADVICE

The questions raised by your letter go to the propriety of a nonprofit, tax-exempt corporation using money which it receives from the "purse pool" for political action purposes. The Political Reform Act does not govern questions of this nature. It is principally a reporting statute. So long as the source of the funds is properly identified and the expenditures are accurately reported, no issues are raised under our statute. However, you may wish to contact the various taxing authorities with respect to the tax-exemption issues raised by your correspondence.

. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified.

Should you conclude that you believe the reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act have been violated, you should address your complaint to the Commission's Enforcement Division.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have basically requested that our agency "investigate" the foregoing situation to determine if the provisions of the Political Reform Act1/ have been violated.

ADVICE

The questions raised by your letter go to the propriety of a nonprofit, tax-exempt corporation using money which it receives from the "purse pool" for political action purposes. The Political Reform Act does not govern questions of this nature. It is principally a reporting statute. So long as the source of the funds is properly identified and the expenditures are accurately reported, no issues are raised under our statute. However, you may wish to contact the various taxing authorities with respect to the tax-exemption issues raised by your correspondence.

. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified.

Should you conclude that you believe the reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act have been violated, you should address your complaint to the Commission's Enforcement Division.
embedding.first_500_words: The letter is from the California Fair Political Practices Commission to Christo D. Bardis, an attorney at law in Sacramento, CA. The Commission responds to Mr. Bardis' request for advice regarding the Western Standardbred Association (WSA), which is described as a nonprofit Delaware Corporation doing business in California. The Commission notes that the California Horse Racing Board recognizes the WSA as the exclusive bargaining agent for the Standardbred Racing (Harness) Industry. Since 1983, the WSA has withdrawn monies from the purse pool for political purposes. The Commission references a provision in the WSA's Articles of Incorporation that states: "No part of the net earnings of the corporation shall inure to the benefit of or be distributable to its members, directors, officers, or other private persons, except that the corporation shall be authorized and empowered to pay reasonable compensation for services rendered and to make payments and distributions in furtherance of the purposes set forth in Article Third hereof. No substantial part of the activities of the corporation shall be the carrying on of propaganda, or otherwise attempting, to influence legislation, and the corporation shall not participate in, or intervene in (including the publishing or distribution of statements) any political campaign on behalf of any candidate for public office. Notwithstanding any other provision of these articles, the corporation shall not carry on any other activities not permitted to be carried on by a corporation exempt from Federal income tax under" This provision indicates that the WSA is restricted from using its earnings for political activities, including campaigning on behalf of candidates for public office. section 501(c)(6) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1954 (or the corresponding provision of any future United States Internal Revenue Law).' In July of 1984, as a Director of that corporation, I forced the Board to remove that provision. To date I have been unsuccessful in getting the WSA to refrain from taking money from the purse pool and giving the monies to a self-serving pack called the Harness Horsemen's Political Action Committee, who in turn distribute the funds to politicians. I find this conduct appalling and in violation of the First Amendment. These contributions are not voluntary, they are taken from the purse pool prior to distribution to owners thereby making the contribution tax deductible. The California Business and Professional Code, Chapter 4, Division 8, Section 19613(c) allows deductions from the purse pool for 'administrative expenses and services rendered' with respect to conduct of racing meetings. These expenditures made are not for administrative expenses and services rendered as it relates to a race meeting and, therefore, may be illegal and in violation of statutes that govern your organization. QUESTION You have basically requested that our agency "investigate" the foregoing situation to determine if the provisions of the Political Reform Act1/ have been violated. ADVICE The questions raised by your letter go to the propriety of a nonprofit, tax-exempt corporation using money which it receives from the "purse pool" for political action purposes. The Political Reform Act
